title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
description:
## Longest Increasing Subsequence

Given an integer array `nums`, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.

A subsequence is derived from an array by deleting some (or no) elements without changing the order of the remaining elements.

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= nums.length <= 2500`
- `-10^4 <= nums[i] <= 10^4`

examples.explanation: The longest increasing subsequence is [2,3,7,101] or [2,5,7,101], length 4.
No two elements are strictly increasing.

solutions.label: Dynamic Programming — O(n²)
solutions.explanation: `dp[i]` stores the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at index `i`. For each `i`, look back at all previous indices `j` where `nums[j] < nums[i]` and extend that subsequence. The answer is the maximum value in the dp array.

solutions.label: Binary Search (Patience Sorting) — O(n log n)
solutions.explanation: Maintain a `tails` array where `tails[i]` is the smallest possible tail of any increasing subsequence of length `i+1`. For each number, binary search for its position in `tails`: if it's larger than all tails, extend; otherwise replace the first tail that is ≥ num. The length of `tails` at the end is the LIS length. Note: `tails` itself may not be a valid subsequence, but its length is always correct.

hints: Define dp[i] as the length of the LIS ending at index i. For each i, look back at all j < i where nums[j] < nums[i].
For O(n log n): maintain an array of the smallest possible tail values for subsequences of each length, and use binary search to update it.
